1: The chapter is dated to Jehoiakim's reign — historically earlier than the surrounding chapters (605-597 BCE vs. 587 BCE for ch. 34). The retrospective placement serves a theological purpose: the Rechabites' faithfulness is the immediate contrast to Jerusalem's unfaithfulness in ch. 34. Jeremiah is constructing a case by contrast.
2: lek el-beit haRechabim vedibarta otam vahave'otam beit YHWH: 'Go to the house of the Rechabites, speak with them, and bring them to the house of the LORD, into one of the inner rooms, and offer them wine to drink.' The Rechabites were a nomadic clan descended from Jonadab son of Rechab (2 Kgs 10:15-23) who refused wine, houses, and agriculture as a covenant commitment to a nomadic, austere form of life. Jeremiah is instructed to test their obedience publicly by offering them the very thing their ancestor prohibited — in the temple itself, before witnesses.
3: The full genealogy of Jaazaniah — four generations named — establishes the lineage and historicity of the Rechabite household. The detail signals that this is a real family with a documented ancestral tradition, not a hypothetical case study.
4: The specific chamber (lishkat benei Hanan ben-Igdalyahu ish ha-elohim, the chamber of Hanan son of Igdaliah, the man of God) is noteworthy: a chamber associated with a prophetic figure ('the man of God') was the location of the test. Adjacent to the officials' chambers and above a priestly chamber — the setting is at the intersection of prophetic, official, and priestly spaces, maximizing the public and institutional nature of the test.
5: va-eten lifnei benei beit haRechabim gevi'im melaim yayin veqosot va-omar aleichem shetu yayin: 'Then I placed before the members of the Rechabite household pitchers full of wine and cups, and I said to them: Drink wine.' The test is entirely formal: Jeremiah personally offers the wine in an authoritative setting. The Rechabites' refusal therefore cannot be attributed to social awkwardness or lack of opportunity — it is a deliberate, articulate response to a direct invitation from a prophet in the temple.
6: vayomru lo nishteh yayin ki Yonadav ben-Rechav avinu tzivanu lemor lo tishteu yayin atem uvneichem ad-olam (wayyōmĕrû lōʾ nište yayin kî yônādāb ben-rēḵāb ʾābînû ṣiwwānû lēmōr lōʾ tišĕtû-yayin ʾattem ûbĕnekem ʿad-ʿôlām): 'But they replied: We will not drink wine, for Jonadab son of Rechab, our father, commanded us: You shall not drink wine, you or your sons, ever.' The response is immediate and communal — the entire household speaks with one voice. The reference to their ancestor's command (tzivanu) as authoritative is the key: they are not deciding in the moment but citing an inherited, binding obligation. Ad-olam (forever/in perpetuity) — the command had no sunset clause; it was meant to define Rechabite identity across generations.
7: uvayit lo tivnu vezera lo tizre'u vekharem lo titaa velo yihyeh lachem: 'You shall not build a house, sow seed, or plant a vineyard or own one. You shall live in tents all your days.' The Rechabite lifestyle was a deliberate rejection of the settled agricultural economy that Israel had adopted in Canaan and that was associated with Baal worship (the fertility cult). By refusing houses, vineyards, and cultivation, the Rechabites embodied a counter-cultural testimony to the nomadic covenant-relationship with YHWH, deliberately maintained against the dominant culture. The motivation (lema'an tichyu yamim rabbim al-pnei ha-adamah asher atem garim sham, so that you may live long in the land where you are strangers) makes longevity in the land contingent on faithfulness — the same structure as the covenant's land-tenure conditions.
8: The collective voice of the Rechabites: vanishma beqol Yonadav ben-Rechav avinu lechol asher tzivanu (we have obeyed the voice of Jonadab son of Rechab, our father, in everything he commanded us). The contrast with Israel's 'we did not listen' (lo shama'nu, v. 14-17) is the entire point: a human ancestor commanded, and his descendants obeyed for generations; YHWH the Creator commanded, and his covenant people persistently disobeyed.
9: The specific enumeration of what they have NOT done — not built houses, not owned vineyards or fields or seeds — follows the ancestral commands of v. 7 point by point. The listing is deliberate: each negative corresponds to a cultural norm they have refused. The Rechabite testimony is structured as a covenant affirmation: here is the command, and here is what we have done and not done in response.
10: vaneshev ba-ohalim venishma vanase'ah lechol asher tzivanu Yonadab avinu: 'We have lived in tents and have obeyed and done all that our father Jonadab commanded us.' The summary claim: full and comprehensive obedience. The tents are the physical sign of their covenant identity — portable, impermanent, dependent on YHWH rather than on landed security. The wilderness ideal maintained into the era of urban civilization.
11: vayhi bia'lot Nevukhadreatzar melech Bavel al-ha-aretz vanomer bo vanavo Yerushalaim: 'But when Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon came up against the land, we said: Come, let us go to Jerusalem for fear of the Chaldean army and the Syrian army. That is why we are living in Jerusalem now.' The exception proves the rule: even the Rechabites, bound by their ancestor's nomadic ideal, had to take refuge in a city under existential military threat. The concession is practical and explicitly temporary — they are 'living in Jerusalem now,' not permanently settling. The crisis has forced a tactical retreat from their lifestyle, but not from their values.
12: The messenger formula introducing the theological application. YHWH now draws the lesson from the Rechabite test — the observation of their obedience becomes the indictment of Israel's disobedience.
13: koh amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael lech ve-amarta el-anshei Yehudah ve-el yoshevei Yerushalaim halo tiqchu musar lishmo'a el-devarai neum YHWH: 'Go and say to the people of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem: Will you not receive instruction and listen to my words?' Musar (instruction/discipline/correction) is the wisdom term for the teachable reception of correction. The Rechabites received and implemented their father's musar; Jerusalem refuses YHWH's. The rhetorical question (halo tiqchu musar) expects the answer 'yes, you should' but acknowledges by implication the answer has been 'no.'
14: huqam mitzvat Yonadav ben-Rechav asher tzivah et-banav levilti shtot yayin ve'azoh lo shatu ad-hayom hazeh ki shamu et-mitzvat avihem: 'The command that Jonadab son of Rechab gave to his sons — to drink no wine — has been kept. To this day they drink none, because they have obeyed their ancestor's command. But I have spoken to you persistently, and you have not listened to me.' The pivotal contrast: Jonadab commanded; they obeyed. YHWH spoke persistently (hashkem vediber, rising early and speaking — the idiom for urgent, repeated communication); they did not listen. The theological force: a human ancestor commands more effectively than the divine Creator. The question implicit in the contrast: why? The answer Jeremiah's book returns to repeatedly: the stubborn, rebellious heart (lev sorer u-moreh, 5:23).
15: va-eshlach aleichem et-kol-avadai haneviim hashkem ve-shloch lemor shuvu na ish midarcho hara-ah: 'I have sent you all my servants the prophets, again and again, saying: Turn now, each of you, from your evil way.' The phrase hashkem veshalloach (rising early and sending) — the urgency-idiom for repeated, insistent communication (cf. 7:13, 25; 11:7; 25:3-4; 26:5; 29:19; 32:33; 44:4). YHWH has been 'early and persistent' in sending prophets; Israel has been persistent in not listening. The contrast between YHWH's urgency and Israel's indifference is one of Jeremiah's central theological themes.
16: vayaqimu benei Yonadav ben-Rechav et-mitzvat avihem asher tzivam veha-am hazeh lo shamu elai: 'The descendants of Jonadab son of Rechab have kept their father's command, but this people has not obeyed me.' The direct comparison. The indictment is stark in its brevity: eight words in Hebrew. The Rechabites' multi-generational obedience to a human ancestor versus Israel's persistent disobedience to the divine Father who gave them the covenant from Sinai.
17: lachen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael hineni mevi el-Yehudah ve-el-kol yoshevei Yerushalaim et kol-hara-ah asher dibarti aleichem: 'Therefore thus says the LORD, the God of hosts, the God of Israel: I am going to bring on Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem all the disaster I have pronounced against them.' The logical consequence is stated without elaboration — the argument of vv. 13-16 has made it self-evident. The comprehensive titles (YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael) frame the judgment as both universal sovereign and covenant-specific God: the disaster comes not from an impersonal force but from the God who is personally invested in the covenant.
18: uveit haRechabim amar Yirmiyahu koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael ya-an asher shema'tem et-mitzvat Yonadav avichem: 'Because you have obeyed the command of Jonadab your father and kept all his instructions and done all that he commanded you.' The threefold statement of obedience — shematem (listened to), tishmerou (kept), ta-asu (did) — hearing, retention, and action: the complete circuit of covenantal obedience. Each element represents a dimension of faithfulness: hearing the command, retaining it across generations, and enacting it in practice.
19: lachen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael lo-yikaret ish liYonadav ben-Rechav omed lefanai kol-hayamim (lākēn kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-yikkārēt ʾîš lĕyônādāb ben-rēḵāb ʿōmēd lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm): 'Therefore thus says the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel: Jonadab son of Rechab shall never fail to have a man standing before me.' Omed lefanai (standing before me) is the covenant phrase for priestly and prophetic service (Deut 10:8; 1 Kgs 17:1; Jer 15:19). The Rechabites are promised perpetual covenantal standing — the same guarantee given to the Davidic line (33:17) and the Levitical priesthood (33:18). Faithfulness to covenant obligations is rewarded with covenantal continuity.
